metadata.purpose: DB-load staging for M15-C VCG cleanup. Two operations: (1) create new VCGs + assign verses + mark anchors; (2) set-aside verses with the file's no_inner_being_phenomenon reason.

vcg_creations.label: God's omniscient knowing (his attribute)
vcg_creations.context_description: The term names God's comprehensive, self-sufficient knowing of all things — hearts, plans, character, deeds. No one gave him this knowing; his understanding is unsearchable. Distinct from his elective knowing of persons (VCG02) and his redemptive self-disclosure through acts (VCG03). Inner-being phenomenon: divine omniscient attribute.

anchor_proposed.anchor_rationale: God's knowing of the psalmist stated in its most personal and overwhelming form: 'Such knowledge is too wonderful for me; it is high; I cannot attain it.' The verse names the attribute (God's knowing of the inner person), its quality (too wonderful to comprehend), and its effect (the human person cannot reach or contain it). The phenomenon — omniscient divine knowing — is stated at its most direct.

vcg_creations.label: God's elective / covenantal knowing of persons
vcg_creations.context_description: The term names God's knowing as an act of electing, choosing, and entering into intimate covenantal relationship — face to face knowing, knowing in the wilderness, 'You only have I known of all the families of the earth.' This is not God's omniscient knowing of everyone but his specific relational knowing that constitutes covenant identity. Inner-being phenomenon: divine elective knowing.

anchor_proposed.anchor_rationale: 'You only have I known of all the families of the earth; therefore I will punish you for all your iniquities.' The most concentrated statement of election-as-knowing in Scripture. The verb's force is completely clear: knowing = choosing = entering covenant relationship. Its consequence (accountability) shows the weight of what this knowing establishes.

vcg_creations.label: God making himself known through acts
vcg_creations.context_description: The term names God's redemptive self-disclosure through historical events — 'that you/they may know that I am the Lord.' The pattern runs through Exodus, the wilderness, the Jordan crossing, the prophets, and the apocalyptic oracles. The goal is always inner recognition produced by the event. Inner-being phenomenon: God's self-revealing disclosure through history.

anchor_proposed.anchor_rationale: The explicit statement of God's purpose in the Jordan crossing: 'so that all the peoples of the earth may know that the hand of the Lord is mighty, that you may fear the Lord your God forever.' Names the event, the intended inner knowing it will produce, the scope (all peoples), and the relational goal (fear = covenant orientation). The pattern of the entire VCG stated in a single verse.

vcg_creations.label: Human covenantal knowing of God
vcg_creations.context_description: The term names the human person's relational inner knowing of God — pressing on to know him, finding the knowledge of God, knowing him in the act of doing justice. This is the human side of the covenantal encounter: the person's inner recognition of who God is, sustained by covenant practice. Inner-being phenomenon: human covenantal knowledge of God.

anchor_proposed.anchor_rationale: 'Let us know; let us press on to know the Lord; his going out is sure as the dawn.' The most direct statement of human covenantal pursuit of knowing God. The verb is active — pressing on, seeking — and the object is the Lord himself as a relational reality. The simile (dawn, spring rain) names the certainty and fruitfulness of what this inner pursuit will encounter.

vcg_creations.label: Covenantal absence: not knowing God
vcg_creations.context_description: The term names the structural opposite — a person, community, or nation that does not know God, refuses the knowledge of God, or lacks it. The sons of Eli did not know the Lord. Israel has no knowledge of God in the land. The wicked do not desire the knowledge of his ways. Sometimes the absence is culpable refusal; sometimes it is the condition of those outside the covenant. Inner-being phenomenon: absent or refused covenantal knowing.

anchor_proposed.anchor_rationale: 'My people are destroyed for lack of knowledge; because you have rejected knowledge, I reject you from being a priest to me.' The most explicit statement of covenantal ignorance's consequence: destruction, priestly rejection, divine forgetting of the next generation. The verse names the absence (lack of knowledge), the act behind it (rejection), and the catastrophic result. The full weight of not knowing God is stated here.

vcg_creations.label: da.at as inner content and capacity held by the person
vcg_creations.context_description: The term names knowledge as an inner content or capacity possessed, accumulated, guarded, and transmitted — the Proverbs portrait of da.at as a valued inner endowment. The wise lay up knowledge; lips of the wise spread it; knowledge is pleasant to the soul; the prudent guard it. Includes knowledge as the content of wise speech, as God's creative-ordering capacity, and as the transmissible substance of wisdom instruction. Inner-being phenomenon: knowledge as inner content held and exercised.

anchor_proposed.anchor_rationale: 'The fear of the Lord is the beginning of knowledge; fools despise wisdom and instruction.' Names knowledge as having a covenantal ground (fear of the Lord), a positive possessor (those who begin with this), and a structural opponent (fools who despise it). The whole Proverbs portrait of da.at as constituted inner capacity is implied in this programmatic statement.

vcg_creations.label: Knowledge of good and evil (moral-discriminating knowing)
vcg_creations.context_description: The term names the specifically moral knowing that distinguishes good from evil — named in the tree of the knowledge of good and evil, acquired through the fall, withheld from moral infants, and called for as a developmental marker. This is knowing as a moral-discriminating capacity: the person who can distinguish right from wrong, choose the good, refuse the evil. Inner-being phenomenon: moral discriminating knowledge.

anchor_proposed.anchor_rationale: 'But of the tree of the knowledge of good and evil you shall not eat, for in the day that you eat of it you shall surely die.' The naming of the prohibited inner capacity. The tree is defined by what it contains: knowledge of good and evil as a transformative moral orientation. The prohibition reveals what is at stake — this knowledge is not information but a capacity that will reshape the inner person, with consequences.

vcg_creations.label: Settled inner conviction (knowing as certainty held within)
vcg_creations.context_description: The term names knowing as a settled inner conviction — the 'we know that' pattern of the Pauline letters; Job's sustained convictions about God, his situation, and his Redeemer; Qoheleth's recognitions about what he has perceived. The phenomenon is not the relational knowing of God (VCG04) but the inner state of holding something as certain. Inner-being phenomenon: inner certainty or conviction.

anchor_proposed.anchor_rationale: 'And we know that for those who love God all things work together for good.' The most recognisable instance of the oida-conviction pattern: a settled inner knowing held by the community, grounding their orientation amid suffering. The 'we know that' form is the paradigm of the group — knowledge as an inner certainty that shapes how life is navigated.

vcg_creations.label: Culpable ignorance (agnoeō and equivalents)
vcg_creations.context_description: The term names ignorance that is culpable, structural, or judicially significant — the agnoeō (G0050) pattern: those who do not know because they have not perceived, those whose ignorance becomes the mechanism of their actions, those Paul corrects by disclosure. Includes innocent ignorance where the absence rather than the culpability is what the term names. Inner-being phenomenon: inner state of ignorance with moral or judicial significance.

anchor_proposed.anchor_rationale: 'For, being ignorant of the righteousness of God, and seeking to establish their own, they did not submit to God's righteousness.' The most theologically weighted instance of agnoeō: ignorance of God's righteousness is the inner root of the whole pattern of self-righteous religious effort. Names the content of the ignorance (God's righteousness), the alternative inner pursuit it produces (self-righteousness), and the consequence (non-submission).

vcg_creations.label: Practical and experiential knowing
vcg_creations.context_description: The term names the ordinary, practical knowing of persons, facts, situations, and skills — knowing where someone went, knowing that a person is trustworthy, knowing a skill, recognising someone. This is knowing as everyday awareness and competence, not covenantal or moral. The largest group by verse count, spanning narrative throughout Genesis–Malachi and the NT. Inner-being phenomenon: practical inner awareness and recognition.

anchor_proposed.anchor_rationale: 'And the dove came back to him in the evening, and behold, in her mouth was a freshly plucked olive leaf. So Noah knew that the waters had subsided from the earth.' The phenomenon is unmistakably clear: inference from evidence produces inner knowledge of a fact about the world. The term names the cognitive movement from sign to certainty — simple, practical, grounded. A clean instantiation of the phenomenon without covenantal or moral overlay.

vcg_creations.label: Sexual knowing (intimate union expressed as knowing)
vcg_creations.context_description: The term names sexual union through the verb 'to know' — the Hebraic expression of the deepest intimacy between persons. Includes positive descriptions of marital union, statements of virginity ('had not known a man'), and instances of sexual violation. The phenomenon is intimate union — the most personal form of human knowing — expressed through the same verb that names all other forms of knowing. Inner-being phenomenon: intimate union as knowing.

anchor_proposed.anchor_rationale: 'Now Adam knew Eve his wife, and she conceived and bore Cain.' The paradigmatic use: intimate union expressed as knowing, with its fruit immediately named. The verse makes the connection between knowing and the deepest human relational act as natural and unremarkable — which is precisely what shows how fundamental the verb is to the inner-being category of knowing.

vcg_creations.label: Eschatological knowing (earth filled with knowledge of God)
vcg_creations.context_description: The term names the universal knowing of God that marks the end of history — 'the earth shall be full of the knowledge of the Lord as the waters cover the sea.' This is not the present covenantal knowing of the faithful few but the future universal inner knowing of all creation. Inner-being phenomenon: eschatological universal knowing.

anchor_proposed.anchor_rationale: 'For the earth will be filled with the knowledge of the glory of the Lord as the waters cover the sea.' The most complete statement of the eschatological knowing: universal (the whole earth), thorough (as waters cover the sea), glorious in content (knowledge of God's glory). The comparison with the covering waters names total saturation — no part of creation excluded.

setasides.context_description: Verses where the M15-C term applies to inanimate objects (mountains not knowing when overturned; the dawn knowing its place; Job 35:15 — God's alleged inattention as a challenged claim) or where the term names a phenomenon that lies outside the inner-being scope of M15-C.
